Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Off Spin, averaging 85.5 kph (up to 93.4).
  • Typical length is 4-5m (their good length); median 4.2 m.
  • Stock ball is full on the stumps, turning away, drifting in, hitting the stumps (31% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 97% of the time against left-handers.
  • Turns it 4.4° on average; 33% of balls turn ≥5°.
  • Across an over he repeats his length ball after ball.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Good Length (4 wkts, 1.9% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: 5th stump.
  • Takes 33% of wickets pitching full on the stumps.
  • Beats the bat 4.2% of tracked balls (false-shot 10.5%).
  • Gets you bowled more than most — 40% of his wickets (2.4× the typical rate for this type).
  • 57% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, bowler, slip: 1st.
  • Very repeatable with his length — using your feet to change his length is more effective than waiting for a loose ball.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching full on the stumps (23% of runs).
  • Away from good length, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak through the leg side (44%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes good length in the channel (economy 4.9, beats the bat only 10%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs left-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — full on the stumps, turning away, drifting in, hitting the stumps (31% of deliveries, economy 2.19). Minor variations: full in the channel (17%) and a good length outside off (12%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %

full in the channel ▶turning away, drifting in17%1.77110%

full outside off ▶turning away, drifting in12%3.29213%
full down the leg side ▶turning away, drifting in9%3.05110%
a good length in the channel ▶turning away, drifting in9%4.94110%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs left-handers)

45% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 20 balls; most runs go to the leg side (44%); the cut hurts most (8 fours/sixes at 2.3 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at stumps.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the off side (53%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it full on the stumps most often (29%); rarely drops short (0%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched full on the stumps, but strikes most often pitching on the 5th stump.

Speed & Spells

He drops ~2.1 km/h from his opening spell to later spells, keeps his pace into the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.

Sequencing & Over Construction

Relentlessly consistent — repeats his length ball after ball (length spread ±0.9 m; more repeatable than 79% of spin bowlers); holds a steady length right through the over; and holds the same crease position through the over.
Often strikes with a shorter ball than the previous one (22% of dismissals).
Across 31 dismissals, his wicket ball is close in length and pace to the delivery before it — he builds pressure with repetition, not a big change-up.

Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
